fuzzy_prd:
hey team, we need to ship the distributed tracing toolkit we've been talking about. basically the pain point is that every service team is rolling their own header parsing and sampling logic and they keep breaking each other's traces because nothing lines up end-to-end. we had that incident last quarter where the checkout service dropped 40% of traces because their hex encoding was subtly wrong compared to what the auth service expected — that kind of thing.

the core pieces we need: some way to pack and unpack the trace context into a single header string (remember how we handled the sign/unsigned thing in that payments codec? do something consistent like that), a few different sampling strategies including one that gives a guaranteed floor so low-traffic operations still show up, a token bucket thing for rate controlling the volume (needs to work with a fake clock for testing), some small helpers for IP addresses and metadata keys, support for the B3 headers that zipkin folks use, the text-map propagation config, a metrics backend for counting spans, and a baggage policy checker.

please keep things modular — last time someone jammed everything into one file and it was a nightmare. errors should be clean categories not raw exception dumps. ping me if you have questions on the sampling math or the wire format details.

trigger_keywords: how should negative trace ids be encoded
what format for signed 64-bit ids in the wire string
how to serialize negative span id to hex
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Negative signed 64-bit ids must be rendered as their full unsigned two's-complement lowercase hex representation. For example, the signed value -10 encodes as 'fffffffffffffff6'. The flags byte is rendered as its unsigned hex value (0 through ff). The four fields are joined by colons with no zero-padding on the ids — only their natural hex length is used.

trigger_keywords: how does context decode handle negative values
what signed type does the flags field parse back to
round-trip behavior for flags byte above 127
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When decoding, each of the three 64-bit id fields is parsed as a signed 64-bit integer, so 'fffffffffffffff6' round-trips back to -10. The flags field is parsed as a signed byte, so hex '81' (decimal 129 unsigned) decodes to -127 as a signed byte. The four output lines are trace_id=, span_id=, parent_id=, flags= each with their signed decimal values.

trigger_keywords: what error is returned for empty context string
how to distinguish empty input from wrong field count in decode
malformed vs empty error codes for wire string parsing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty input string yields exactly 'error=empty_tracer_state_string'. A non-empty string whose colon-separated field count is not exactly four yields 'error=malformed_tracer_state_string' followed by 'value=<raw input>'. These are two distinct normalized error categories — never raw exception text.

trigger_keywords: how is the probabilistic sampler boundary calculated
what threshold does probabilistic sampling use for positive vs negative ids
formula for the probabilistic sampler keep/drop boundary
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a rate r in [0,1]: the positive boundary is floor((2^63 - 1) * r); a positive id is sampled when id <= positive boundary. The negative boundary is floor(-2^63 * r); a negative id is sampled when id >= negative boundary. A rate strictly less than 0 or strictly greater than 1 returns 'error=sampling_rate_out_of_range' followed by 'rate=<value>'.

trigger_keywords: what sampler tags does the guaranteed throughput sampler report
when does guaranteed sampler report lowerbound vs probabilistic type
how does the guaranteed sampler pick which tag type to emit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The guaranteed-throughput sampler first checks the probabilistic component. If the probabilistic sampler would sample the trace, it reports sampler_type=probabilistic with sampler_param=<rate>. Only if the probabilistic component does NOT sample does it fall back to the lower-bound rate limiter, reporting sampler_type=lowerbound with sampler_param=<rate> (the probabilistic rate, not the lower_bound value). The sampler_param for lowerbound uses 5 decimal places (e.g. 0.00010).

trigger_keywords: how does the update mechanism work for guaranteed sampler
when does a guaranteed sampler update return true vs false
what counts as a real change when reconfiguring the guaranteed sampler
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each update in the updates array emits an 'update=true' or 'update=false' line before the final sampling result. An update returns false if both the rate and lower_bound are identical to the current configuration, and true if either value actually changes. Updates are applied in order; after all updates are processed, the sampling decision is taken with the final configuration.

trigger_keywords: how does the token bucket rate limiter start
initial balance of the rate limiter
does the rate limiter bucket start empty or full
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The token-bucket rate limiter starts full, meaning the initial balance equals max_balance. Each step optionally advances a virtual clock via advance_micros and/or advance_millis (both can appear on the same step and their elapsed times are combined). Before attempting the charge, the balance is replenished by elapsed_seconds * credits_per_second, capped at max_balance. A request is admitted (allow=true) only when the current balance >= cost, and the cost is then deducted. If balance < cost, allow=false and the balance is unchanged.

trigger_keywords: what error codes does ip_to_int return
how to distinguish empty ip from invalid ip format
what output for non-dotted-quad ip address
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty string input yields 'error=empty_ip' with no additional line. A non-empty string that is not a valid dotted-quad IPv4 address yields 'error=not_four_octets' followed by 'ip=<raw input>'. A valid dotted-quad yields 'ip_int=<signed 32-bit integer>' where 255.255.255.255 maps to -1 (two's-complement 32-bit).

trigger_keywords: how are B3 inject headers sorted in output
what order are the b3 headers emitted
is the parent span id header always included in b3 inject
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Output lines from b3_inject are sorted alphabetically by header name. The X-B3-ParentSpanId header is emitted ONLY when parent_id is non-zero. All three id fields (TraceId, SpanId, ParentSpanId) are zero-padded to exactly 16 lowercase hex characters. X-B3-Sampled is '1' when flags bit 0 is set, else '0'. When the debug flag is set, X-B3-Flags=1 is also emitted. Baggage items use the prefix 'baggage-' by default.

trigger_keywords: how does b3 extract handle a 128-bit trace id
what happens to a 32-character trace id in b3 extract
how are flags combined from sampled and flags headers in b3 extract
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A 128-bit trace id (32 hex characters) is downgraded to its lower 64 bits (the last 16 hex characters). X-B3-Sampled=1 sets bit 0 of the context flags; X-B3-Flags=1 sets bit 1, so both present yields flags=3. Baggage headers matching the prefix (default 'baggage-') are collected; unrelated headers are ignored. If the required TraceId and SpanId headers are absent, output is 'context=none'.

trigger_keywords: what are the default values for the text-map codec config
what is the default context key and baggage prefix for textmap
textmap propagation default header name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The text-map propagation codec defaults are: context_key='uber-trace-id', baggage_prefix='uberctx-', and url_encoding as configured. All three values are overridable. The config output emits exactly three lines: context_key=, baggage_prefix=, url_encoding= (true or false).

trigger_keywords: what does querying a nonexistent metrics counter return
initial value of a newly created counter or timer or gauge
how do metrics get keyed — by name only or name plus tags
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Metrics are keyed by both name and their exact tag set. Querying a name/tag combination that was never created returns -1. A created counter or timer starts at 0 and accumulates increments (inc) or recorded durations (duration) respectively. A gauge starts at 0 and each 'update' action replaces the current value entirely rather than accumulating.

trigger_keywords: how is the tracer metrics query output formatted
what separator is used between metric name and tags in tracer_metrics output
how are span counters labeled in the tracer metrics output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each query result line in tracer_metrics output has the format: '<metric_name>|<tags_string>=<value>'. The tags string is exactly the comma-separated key=value string provided in the query (e.g. 'sampled=y,state=started'). Starting one sampled span increments jaeger:started_spans with tags 'sampled=y' and jaeger:traces with tags 'sampled=y,state=started' to 1. A query whose tag string does not exactly match any created series returns -1.

trigger_keywords: what does the baggage restriction policy allow by default
is there a length cap on baggage values
does the default baggage policy care about service name or key name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default baggage restriction policy allows every key regardless of the service name or key name queried. Every allowed key has a maximum value length of 2048 characters. Output is two lines: 'key_allowed=true' and 'max_value_length=2048'.

trigger_keywords: how should the rate limiting sampler param be formatted
what decimal format does ratelimiting sampler use for its param
is the ratelimiting sampler param an integer or float in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The rate-limiting sampler reports sampler_param as a decimal with one decimal place, e.g. '123.0' not '123'. The sampler_type is 'ratelimiting'. With a fresh bucket the first request is always admitted (sampled=true).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'context_encode', 'context_decode', 'sampler_const', 'sampler_probabilistic', 'sampler_ratelimiting', 'sampler_guaranteed', 'rate_limiter', 'ip_to_int', 'normalize_baggage_key', 'b3_inject', 'b3_extract', 'textmap_config', 'metrics', 'tracer_metrics', 'baggage_restriction'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled the sign/unsigned thing in that payments codec? do something consistent like that
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The context_encode operation must render all three 64-bit ids as unsigned lowercase hex (two's-complement for negatives, e.g. -10 → fffffffffffffff6) and context_decode must parse them back as signed 64-bit integers, with flags parsed as a signed byte — matching the canonical wire format defined in feature1_1 and feature1_2 test cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: a token bucket thing for rate controlling the volume (needs to work with a fake clock for testing)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The rate limiter must use an explicit virtual clock driven by advance_micros and advance_millis fields in each step, not wall-clock time, so behavior is fully deterministic and reproducible as specified in feature3_rate_limiter.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: one that gives a guaranteed floor so low-traffic operations still show up
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The guaranteed-throughput sampler (sampler_guaranteed op) combines a probabilistic sampler with a lower-bound rate limiter; it reports sampler_type=lowerbound when the probabilistic component does not sample, ensuring at least one sample per interval per operation, as specified in feature2_4_sampler_guaranteed.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: errors should be clean categories not raw exception dumps
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All invalid inputs must produce normalized string error codes: 'empty_tracer_state_string', 'malformed_tracer_state_string', 'sampling_rate_out_of_range', 'empty_ip', 'not_four_octets' — surfaced as 'error=<code>' lines with no stack traces or language runtime exception text.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented — you'll need to look at the existing implementation patterns in the codebase or check with the engineering lead. The key principle is that all behaviors should be deterministic, errors should be normalized category strings, and each concern (propagation, sampling, rate limiting, metrics) should stay in its own module.

feature1_1_context_encode.json.description: Serialize a trace context (trace id, span id, parent id, one-byte flags) into its canonical colon-separated lowercase-hex wire string. Each of the three 64-bit ids is printed as unsigned lowercase hex and the flags byte as its unsigned hex value.

feature1_2_context_decode.json.description: Parse a colon-separated lowercase-hex trace-context wire string back into its four numeric components. The string must contain exactly four colon-separated hex fields; an empty string and a string with the wrong number of fields are reported as distinct normalized errors.

feature2_1_sampler_const.json.description: A constant sampler always returns the same fixed sampling decision regardless of operation name or trace id, and reports its decision via sampler tags (type 'const' and the boolean decision as the parameter).

feature2_2_sampler_probabilistic.json.description: A probabilistic sampler samples a trace when its id falls within a boundary derived from the configured rate in [0,1]. Positive ids sample when id is at or below the positive boundary; negative ids sample when id is at or above the negative boundary. The reported tags carry type 'probabilistic' and the rate. A rate outside [0,1] is a normalized range error.

feature2_3_sampler_ratelimiting.json.description: A rate-limiting sampler reports tags with type 'ratelimiting' and the configured maximum traces-per-second as the parameter. With a fresh bucket the first request is admitted.

feature2_4_sampler_guaranteed.json.description: A guaranteed-throughput sampler combines a probabilistic sampler with a lower-bound rate limiter so that every operation is sampled at least once per interval. When the probabilistic sampler samples, its tags (type 'probabilistic') are reported; otherwise the lower-bound tags (type 'lowerbound') are reported. It can be reconfigured at runtime; an update returns whether anything actually changed.

feature3_rate_limiter.json.description: A token-bucket rate limiter starts full (balance = max). Each step may advance a virtual clock (microseconds and/or milliseconds) before charging a cost; the request is admitted only when the accumulated balance covers the cost. Credits refill at the configured rate but the balance is capped at the configured maximum.

feature4_1_ip_to_int.json.description: Convert a dotted-quad IPv4 address into its signed 32-bit integer value (big-endian, two's-complement). An empty string and a non-IPv4 string are reported as distinct normalized errors.

feature4_2_normalize_baggage_key.json.description: Normalize a baggage key by replacing underscores with hyphens and lowercasing the result.

feature5_1_b3_inject.json.description: Inject a trace context into a B3 multi-header carrier. Each id is written as zero-padded 16-character lowercase hex; the parent header is emitted only when the parent id is non-zero; the sampled header is '1' when sampled, else '0'; debug flag emits a flags header. Baggage items are written under a configurable prefix (default 'baggage-'). Output lines are sorted by header name.

feature5_2_b3_extract.json.description: Extract a trace context from a B3 multi-header carrier. A 128-bit trace id is downgraded to its lower 64 bits. The sampled and flags headers combine into the context flags. Baggage carried under the prefix is collected and unrelated headers are ignored. When the required id headers are absent, no context is produced.

feature6_textmap_config.json.description: A text-map propagation codec exposes its effective configuration: the header key used for the serialized context, the prefix used for baggage headers, and whether values are URL-encoded. Defaults are 'uber-trace-id' and 'uberctx-'; all three are overridable.

feature7_1_metrics_counter.json.description: An in-memory counter is identified by its name plus its tag set. Querying a name/tag combination that was never created returns -1; a created counter starts at 0 and accumulates increments.

feature7_2_metrics_timer.json.description: An in-memory timer is identified by its name plus its tag set. Querying an unknown name/tag combination returns -1; a created timer starts at 0 and accumulates recorded durations.

feature7_3_metrics_gauge.json.description: An in-memory gauge is identified by its name plus its tag set. Querying an unknown name/tag combination returns -1; a created gauge starts at 0 and each update replaces the current value with the new value.

feature8_tracer_span_metrics.json.description: Starting a span through the tracer pipeline emits counters into the metrics backend. Counters are addressed by metric name and a comma-separated tag string. Starting one sampled span increments started_spans(sampled=y) and traces(sampled=y,state=started) to 1 while their not-sampled counterparts stay 0. Querying a counter whose tag string does not exactly match a created series returns -1.

feature9_baggage_restriction.json.description: The default baggage restriction policy allows every key and caps each baggage value length at 2048 characters, independent of the service or key queried.
